speaker: interviewer
text: --husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good story. Working at Michael [? Riese, ?] getting acquainted with a lot of young Jewish women and doctors, I found out that there was a dance at the Shoreham Hotel on a certain Saturday night, sponsored by Habonim, the German Jewish Congregation. And I went with a date that I wasn't really crazy about going with. He was a tool and die maker. And he had tickets to the ice hockey game and a nice car. And it was kind of-- that was good. Didn't-- asked him whether he knew how to dance. He said no, but he would take dancing lessons so I would accept this date. So we went. And we danced. But he danced more on my feet than on the ground. So I thought, this will never do. I excused myself, went to the powder room, powdered my nose, and looked around a little bit. And across a crowded room, I saw a young man that I had been dating. He was from Argentina. And he was there stag with a very good-looking young man. I thought, now there is a good-looking guy. And I crossed the crowded room. And I said hello to my friend. He then had to introduce me to his friend, which became-- who became my husband. We danced. And it was all over right away as far as I was concerned. And just as he took me back to my table, he asked me for my phone number. And I waited anxiously for the call that didn't come. It was only three weeks later-- it wasn't so terrible, because at Michael [? Riese ?] there were a lot of young Jewish doctors. And so dating was not a problem. But three weeks later, he called. By that time, I had forgotten the name. Eiseman, I never had-- that was an unusual name for me. But I recalled fast. And the reason it took so long, because he also liked me very much, and it should-- he didn't want to take me just for a hamburger and for a movie. It was-- had to be something a little more special. And we double-dated with a friend of his who had a car at the time. And we went to the ballet. And that was the beginning. And this is 46 years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHING] So you married when?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in Montevideo. I could never marry without my parents. So I had my bridal gown. It was my sister-in-law's. I wore my sister-in-law's bridal gown. It was changed a little, but it was her gown. And this young man that I had dated was-- was my husband's best man. And Lenny came to Montevideo. And the entire congregation came to the ceremony. There were 800 people there. And my husband always loved the theater and said he always wanted to be on the stage. It's probably the closest he ever came to being on the stage. It was really wild. But then we had a lovely dinner in a small hotel, just for 100 people. and had a fabulous honeymoon because my husband, at the time, was already with an international grain company who had offices all over. So we really had connections in Argentina and Chile, and went to Peru and had a-- really, a six-week honeymoon. To come back, then, to Chicago, where apartments were hard to find-- but my husband also lived with a German Jewish family, had also a room with them. And they found us an apartment on 54th and Hyde Park Boulevard. I think it was the Gaylord Hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we lived there for just a short while. And we were transferred shortly after. Where is your husband from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's from Atlanta, Georgia. And his grand-- his father was born in Atlanta, Georgia. But his grandfather came from Veinau-Baden.

speaker: interviewee
text: So matter of fact, a few years ago we visited. And we found the Eiseman house in Veinau. And we found all the graves of the Eiseman family still. So that was a very meaningful thing. When did you first go back to Germany after the war? [DEEP SIGH]

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- hmm-- my son Robbie was 11. 37-- about 25-- about 25 years ago. I went back with my husband and my parents back, and stayed in a lovely hotel with my parents. And my father took me all around. I saw my old house. I saw my school. I saw where my grandparents had their stores. And it was very nostalgic, very, very nostalgic. The synagogue had been burned down. That wasn't there anymore. But some of the department stores were built back up again. And the cafes that I remember were there. And it was a very, very nostalgic trip. That's when I went back the first time. And then quite a few years later, I went back again because a friend of mine was an exchange teacher in Germany. And that was not a good experience. I felt very clearly in my heart and mind I would never, ever go back because I felt the anti-Semitism was still there. Not so obvious, but I had an uncomfortable feeling. And I won't go-- I won't go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on that second visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened was my friend said to me, you know, we have to speak in front of a group of German people. They're coming. This was in Lubeck. It was right on the border of East and West Germany. It was interesting, because Lubeck was a beautiful little city with gardens and pretty stores. And on the other side was nothing but gray and swamp and-- and it was just a very distinct feeling. This was West Germany, and this was East Germany. It was very dramatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was probably about 10 years ago, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the reunification.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And, yeah, I did not see the wall. I wasn't in Berlin. But it was before the unification, yes. And so we spoke in front of 500 people. They came from all over. I don't know-- ages-- all ages, young, old, in between. And what we spoke about was our lifestyle in the United States. My friend was a divorced woman with three children that she had to bring up, et cetera. And I-- my husband already was successful. And I had an interesting life socially and had done a lot of charity things. And so people should-- we were supposed to tell people the different lifestyles in the United States in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- in the middle of my speech, a lady raises her hand. And she said, I don't understand. If you're an American, how come you speak such good German? And I said, well, I was born in Germany. Well, why are you an American now? And I thought, this is a loaded question. This is a very loaded question. And I only said one word, real quick came to my mind-- Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with that-- I don't know whether it was my imagination or whether it was reality, but I saw all these German faces kind of like dropping. And I just had this feeling, this eerie feeling that--that the ones that are 80 were Nazis. And the ones that are 40 were children of Nazis. And-- and I just had this very bad feeling that--that--that-- that whole thing was still there someplace, not obvious and not on top of things, but within. I felt that way. And I do feel that way now. So that-- I'm done. No more Germany for me. [LAUGHS] Maybe I have to change planes when I go on a trip. But otherwise, no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did a lot in your life in the United States. You've been involved in a number of activities and a number of careers. Can you just run those through real quick?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how quick. [LAUGHING] I'll try. Right away, after I had my first child, my first activity was once a week as a Gray Lady, they called it, in a hospital. That was in Buffalo, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your first child? Who-- which one was your first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Steven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Steven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was born--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buffalo, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?!

speaker: interviewee
text: 43 years ago, 1952. And so that was my very first thing. And then we also started the National-- my sister-in-law and I, in Buffalo, started a chapter of the National Council of Jewish Women in Buffalo, New York. They helped us. They sent people from New York. They helped us establish that. Then we moved to Minneapolis, Minnesota. I was pregnant with my second child, Cathy, who was born in 1955 in Minnesota. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my next-door neighbor then, in Minneapolis-- not really next-door neighbor, but in the neighborhood-- were Joan and Fritz Mondale. Fritz Mondale, now ambassador to Japan, at the time, attorney general of Minnesota. I followed his career as a senator and as a vice president, and became very active politically. I also started a real estate career in Minneapolis. Once I had my third child, I didn't want to stay home all the time anymore. I became a real-- I probably was-- not probably-- I was among the first six female real estate agents in Minnesota. So what I'm telling you is almost 40 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was number three?

speaker: interviewee
text: Number three was Robbie. He was born in 1959, also in Minneapolis. And in Minneapolis, I was active politically and in the symphony and in the Guthrie Theater. I was a docent at the Guthrie Theater. I was a vice president for the junior group of symphony in Minneapolis and sold houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was busy. Oh, I was also very active at the International Center for Students and Visitors at the University of Minnesota. So we had often, Thanksgiving, foreign students from all over the world, obviously because that was close to my heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we moved here. And I became a medical technologist again for a pediatrician and did that for several years. And then I became a tour guide and worked for the city of Chicago-- or for a company in the city of Chicago, did tours in Spanish and German for people from all around the world. And then I was co-chairman of the Crystal Ball in 1982, where we raised a million and a half plus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know. What came after that? I worked for 10 years in fashion for Neiman Marcus. And currently, I'm co-chairing something for the Holocaust Museum in Washington. And there were several things in between. [LAUGHING] So you've been busy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you a couple of philosophical questions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Your second visit to Germany was not a good one.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could it happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please, God, no. Could? Who knows? Who knows? It went-- they went crazy in Bosnia. There are crazy places all over the world. Could it happen in the United States? I-- I don't ever think so, no. But it's something that we can never, ever be lax about, something that we have to make the whole world never forget. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hope and pray it never will happen again. But I guess it could.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Overall, how did your life turn out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Overall, I couldn't ask for a nicer life. I have a wonderful husband. I have extra-special children who are all very good citizens. And they all do worthwhile things. They're all successful. I have a lovely marriage. And we have-- all-- all of us have a very good relationship with each other. And we're very lucky to have wonderful friends, all-- because of my husband's career, all around the country. I was very blessed that my parents still lived to see my oldest son being bar mitzvah, and came from South America. Matter of fact, they came, for 15 years, every summer, and spent six weeks with us. So the relationship between my parents and my children has been just so wonderful. Even though it was not the quantity, but certainly the quality was there. And if God gives us good health, then I'm looking forward to many more wonderful years. There's a lot to be done in this world. And I'm trying to stay active in worthwhile causes and share my good fortune with the rest of the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for telling your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure. These are my grandparents, my mother's father, [? Zali ?] Hildesheimer, and my mother's mother, Johanna Hildesheimer. They were vacationing in Bad Karlsbad, probably around 1930. They were walking in the park there. I spoke about the Seders in my home and how the first part was always given by my grandfather Hildesheimer, my father's-- my mother's father. And this is the yarmulke that he always wore, particularly on Passover and on the High Holidays. It's probably 100 years old at this point. These are my grandparents, my father's parents, [? Yacob ?] Cohen and [? Dora ?] Cohen. And this is when they were probably in their 40s and just had a picture taken for their own private occasions. These are my parents. This is their engagement picture. My mother was 24. My father was 25. They met at a lecture at the University and fell in love, and were in love for 50 years. This is their engagement picture. This is my brother, Kurt, and I. We were all dressed up to go to a birthday party. And he was three and I was five and a half. This is my brother, Kurt, and I after we immigrated to Montevideo, Uruguay. That was around his bar mitzvah time. He was 13. And I was 15 and a half. This is my passport that was issued to me when I was 12 and a half, almost 13 years old, when we left Germany in 1938. Notice a J on the left-hand side. That was stamped into it by the Gestapo or by the SS people in Germany so that we were identified as Jews. This is my picture taken on that day. You see the stamp with a swastika and my name here to the right-hand side. Taken the day we left Germany, December 16, 1938. OK, that is on my eighth birthday, and my little birthday party with my very best friends. I'm in the middle. And to my right is [? Ifyan ?] [? Meil, ?] my very best friend. To my left is Lilo Baer, the little girl that I described that we got the letter that she could come to the United States. In the middle is also a very close friend, [? Hilda ?] [? Dublong. ?] And I know that she went to Auschwitz and she was killed. The other two, I don't know what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Hannah ?] [? Klein ?] and [? Susanna ?] [? Pel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me what happened to the girl on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Ifyan ?] [? Meil? ?] Through a coincidence, I got-- I found her back after 60 years. And I found out where she lived. And my husband and I celebrated our-- it was my 70th birthday-- in Tuscany, Italy. And I saw my friend [? Ifyan ?] [? Meil ?] back after 60 years. This is my friend that you just saw as a little girl. And this is 60 years later in Tuscany, Italy, on her estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it, in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother, Johanna Hildesheimer, my mother's mother, who was issued this passport in December of '38. And she finally left Germany in 1940. She came finally, in 1940, to Montevideo, Uruguay. I like to add that, at that point, in addition to your name, the German government gave you the Jewish name of Sarah. And that is on her passport. There was a conference in Sao Paulo, Brazil, of all German Jewish communities all around South America. And my father was a representative of the German Jewish community of Montevideo, Uruguay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is he in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is right in the middle with the glasses. This is my husband, Leonard. We are married 46 years now. Behind me are my children. Start with Steve, who was born in 1952, Cathy, who was born in 1955, and Robbie, who was born in 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leonard, what is the best thing about being married to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Having someone around who is always positive and always happy. [EVA LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nice. Steve, tell me about a cherished memory you have of your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, my opa, was probably the man who was most responsible for the development of my identity as a Jew, my love of Judaism, its values and traditions. And I remember him telling me stories when I was little about events in his own life. One where he was trying to take a hill in World War I. One when he was returning from the war, almost dead, having been bombed in a foxhole. And one when he arrived in Uruguay with my mom and my uncle and his wife with absolutely nothing, having to start life over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, in his own distinctive way, 'And people ask me, Steven, why are you so religious? And I tell them, no, my friend, I am not religious enough.' Cathy.

speaker: interviewee
text: My memory is more of just the feeling in the home every summer when they would come to visit, because they lived in Montevideo. So they would come for two or three months every summer. And just the-- the life that was in our home and the--the feeling of just the heritage and the tradition of our family was-- was beautiful. And with my grandmother, I just felt her joy with everything that I did so dearly all the time that-- when I waited for a boy to call and the boy would call, she would jump up and down and sing and dance with me. He called! He called! You know, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: So unfortunately, she didn't live long enough for me to turn into a woman. But as a young girl, I just knew that everything wonderful to me was wonderful to her. And that was something very special. Robbie?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, being the youngest, it's-- the memories are always a little bit more singular. I have a memory once of playing cards with my grandmother. And the game we played was called Schwarzer Peter. Well, I come to learn later that that's blackjack. [LAUGHTER] But it's German. And so I learned that I knew how to play blackjack when I was very young, thanks to my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] Thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. It was a pleasure. I had a cry at the end. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Should we have a group hug? [LAUGHTER] [KISS] [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
